immediate: Remove infected tissue and monitor closely.
prevention: Improve airflow and avoid overhead watering.
notes: Use crop-appropriate treatments if symptoms spread.

immediate: No treatment needed. Continue regular watering and monitoring.
prevention: Keep leaves dry when possible and remove debris to prevent future disease.
Use clean tools and avoid working in wet foliage.
notes: Maintain balanced fertilization to keep plants resilient.

immediate: Remove fallen leaves and fruit to reduce inoculum.
Prune for good airflow through the canopy.
prevention: Use resistant varieties where possible.
Apply a labeled fungicide early in the season if needed.
notes: Scab symptoms often appear as olive or dark lesions.

immediate: Remove heavily infected leaves and prune for airflow.
If feasible, remove galls on nearby junipers to reduce spread.
prevention: Apply a labeled fungicide at bud break and during wet periods.
Choose resistant apple varieties where possible.
notes: Rust needs both apple and juniper hosts to complete its cycle.

immediate: Remove infected leaves and avoid splashing water.
Keep foliage dry to slow lesion expansion.
prevention: Mulch to reduce soil splash and rotate crops annually.
Use a labeled fungicide or bactericide as appropriate.
notes: Leaf spots can be fungal or bacterial; monitor closely.

immediate: Remove heavily infected leaves and prune for airflow.
Dispose of infected debris away from the crop.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and reduce leaf wetness duration.
Apply a labeled fungicide if rust persists.
notes: Rust pustules often appear orange or brown on leaf undersides.

immediate: Remove infected leaves and discard away from the field.
Avoid overhead irrigation and improve air circulation.
prevention: Rotate crops and avoid planting in infected soil.
Apply a labeled fungicide preventively in high-risk periods.
notes: Late blight can spread rapidly under cool, wet conditions.

immediate: Remove mummified fruit and infected canes.
Prune to improve airflow and reduce humidity in the canopy.
prevention: Sanitize pruning tools between cuts.
Apply a labeled fungicide at early fruit set if pressure is high.
notes: Black rot often worsens after warm, wet weather.

immediate: Increase ventilation and reduce leaf wetness.
Remove severely infected leaves.
prevention: Avoid high humidity in enclosed spaces.
Use resistant varieties where available.
notes: Leaf mold thrives in cool, humid environments.

immediate: Remove lower infected leaves to slow spread.
Mulch to reduce soil splash onto foliage.
prevention: Avoid overhead watering and provide good spacing.
Use a labeled fungicide if symptoms continue.
notes: Septoria often starts on lower leaves first.

immediate: Remove infected plants early to limit spread.
Control whiteflies using traps or labeled treatments.
prevention: Use reflective mulch to deter whiteflies.
Install insect netting in high-pressure areas.
notes: Symptoms include upward leaf curl and yellowing.

immediate: Remove infected plants to prevent spread.
Disinfect tools and hands after handling affected plants.
prevention: Control insect vectors and avoid tobacco exposure around tomatoes.
Use certified virus-free seed or transplants.
notes: Viruses cannot be cured; management focuses on prevention.

immediate: Spray the undersides of leaves with water to dislodge mites.
Use insecticidal soap or horticultural oil as labeled.
prevention: Reduce dust and water stress to discourage mite outbreaks.
Encourage beneficial predators when possible.
notes: Check for fine webbing and stippling on leaves.
